Description

#F4F5F7

In a sentence or two, describe the purpose of this space.

Search this Space

Filter by Label

These are all the labels in use in this space. Select a label to see a list of content the label has been applied to.

Recently updated content

This list below will automatically update each time somebody in your space creates or updates content.

page,whiteboard,database,blog10concisetrue

User Structure

  • Username and password must be securely encrypted and support 2FA

  • Picture, first name, last name, phone number, email, teamsid, allowsms, allowteams, isowner

  • Email and phone number must be verified

  • Create a temporary login page

  • Create user can be called from login. First user is the owner.

  • There is only one owner, it's a special user

Development Methodology - AI Driven

  • Start a codex session

  • Identify the next item to work on from the Design order section

  • Create a JIRA Epic for each Codex initial request

  • Create a JIRA task associated with that epic for each code change

  • Create automated testing for every request

  • Create a confluence page explaining what was done, relevant screenshots and how it might benefit MSPs.

  • Human in the loop verification and testing, with possible iterative requirements, added as a story.

  • Update the confluence, human approves,

  • Update Github

  • Provide notes for future codex sessions that would be beneficial

  • codex session completed.

Prompt

note

You are developing a platform application for MSP’s.

  1. An overview of the vision and plan can be found in c:\source\platform\plan\

  2. Context notes from previous sessions can be found under C:\source\platform\context

  3. Log your work under C:\source\platform\logs into CSV’s that can be imported into Jira.

  4. Create unit testing and automation as needed

  5. Document your work under C:\source\platform\documentation so that it can be imported into confluence.

  6. Keep context notes for future iterations under C:\source\platform\context

Your current assignment is to develop:

[Insert Assignment]

You are developing a platform application for MSP’s.

  1. An overview of the vision and plan can be found in c:\source\platform\plan\

  2. Context notes from previous sessions can be found under C:\source\platform\context

  3. Log your work under C:\source\platform\logs into CSV’s that can be imported into Jira.

  4. Create unit testing and automation as needed

  5. Document your work under C:\source\platform\documentation so that it can be imported into confluence.

  6. Keep context notes for future iterations under C:\source\platform\context

Your current assignment is to develop:

[Insert Assignment]

Vision

We are creating a MSP SaaS platform that has AI at its foundation. Thie main pillars of this platform include:

  • Highly customizable. Customers must be able to create their own tables and fields to support their own business. The UI must be flexible in allowing the customer to display information as they see fit and to develop no code lines of business content.

  • Notifications. A quick way to let a user know important information.

  • Community. Sharing securely is important. We assume a peer model where communities can come together andd share their fields and customizations. Vendors are a type of community. It is expected that users may have multiple access to different systems with different permisssions in each.

  • Security and compliance. RBAC permissions, AuthZ, GDAP and other compliance needs for EU, AU, NA are built in. Multilingual and internationalization are built in. Audit trails are built in. Versioning is built in. Retention is built in.

  • AI. Data should be vectorized natively. Expect conversational NLP to be native in the platform. AI should be accessible in copilot, teams, and elsewhere.

  • Automation. Data triggers should kick off a workflow engine that allows a customer to automate processes. A playbook / runbook approach should be used to define a manual flowfirst, and then to automate peices of it. Camunda, N8N are good examples of workflow engines. Should connect to everything microsoft, email, sharepoint, teams.

  • Monitoring. Customers will sell software and solutions to their customers. We must be able to remotely access the system to install software, support it, and ensure its health.

  • End user AI and chat. We will also deliver end user NLP for an MSP's clients

  • Easy to integrate. REST APIs, MCPs are always delivered and support customizations. Spreadsheet and other data imports are common and should be a foundational task customers can do. Vendor API's should be easily imported and usable.

  • Built in coding. Screens can be created using YAML and Python. Backend services can created this way as well. Everything will be reviewed by AI for standards and security. Versioning is built in.

  • Built in dashboarding and reporting. Dashboards are meant to see live data, and navigate to screens. Reporting is deep analysis, often involving month over month and trends.

  • Built in benchmarking. Data in aggregate means you can compare your performance against those of others.

  • Built in segmentation and classification. Customization is possible, but must fall within certain standards. for example, Ticket status can have many names, but falls into New, In Progress, and Done. Healthcare represents and industry. This strategy is to ensure benchmarking works.

  • Bring your user. Users can access multiple MSP’s, and bring their skills and awards with them. gamification to some degree.

  • Consumption based billing. Pay for what you use. But we need to highly explain compute, storage, tokens, and other charges on demand.

  • Global Search. Can be done with traditional keyword or by asking questions

Design order

  1. https://sdemulling.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/AI/pages/2424833/Environment?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiMWNhZmZlOWJhMGEwNDk0N2JhMjQ5ZmQ1NzBhYWNhYjQiLCJwIjoiYyJ9

  3. https://sdemulling.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/AI/pages/2293773/Users?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiYjNlMDA1MWJmNDlhNDU1NzgyYmU2YTgzZmJlYTc3MmUiLCJwIjoiYyJ9

  4. https://sdemulling.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/AI/pages/2424855/Provisioning?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiYzBjOGM1Yjk4YjIxNDJiMTg0MzMxY2E0ZWJlYzU5OTMiLCJwIjoiYyJ9

  5. https://sdemulling.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/AI/pages/2392084/Views+Customization?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiZWFlYmJmYzNhODg1NDU0YTllODg5MTg2ODFjYTEyODEiLCJwIjoiYyJ9

This is a fantastically ambitious and well-structured vision, and honestly, it reads like an evolved blueprint for the “Next-Gen ConnectWise Platform.” It’s modular, highly extensible, AI-native, and clearly written from real product pain points you’ve lived through. Below is a structured critique and a set of enhancements that could increase your chance of long-term success, technically, organizationally, and strategically.

🧭 Overall Vision Feedback

Strengths

  • MSP-centric clarity: You’ve captured the realities of multi-tenant MSP environments (franchises, opcos, multi-MSP identities) beautifully. This will resonate immediately with the ecosystem.

  • Data-centric + AI-native: Vectorization, conversational NLP, and integrated automation show that AI isn’t a bolt-on — it’s foundational.

  • Composable extensibility: The “custom tables via YAML” model is brilliant for building a PaaS-like MSP system.

  • Human-in-the-loop AI ops: The Codex-driven dev workflow bridges AI-generated work and human QA — perfect for iterative safety.

Potential Risks

  • Platform scope creep — you’re essentially combining Notion, ServiceNow, ConnectWise, and Retool into one AI-native platform. To make it real, you’ll need a strong MVP slice.

  • Complexity of schema versioning + customization — even ServiceNow struggles here. Dynamic schema updates, YAML validation, and vector indexing together will be tricky.

  • AI safety + hallucination risk — “AI reviewing AI-generated code” and “AI defining schemas” require strict guardrails (LLM self-review loops + unit tests won’t be enough alone).

🧩 Architecture Critique & Recommendations

1. Data Model (YAML Schema Design)

Critique:
YAML-defined schemas are powerful, but dynamic migrations and tenant isolation require absolute rigor.

Suggestions:

  • Implement a Schema Controller service (like Kubernetes CRDs): validates, version-controls, and applies YAML schemas via APIs.

  • Store schema versions in Git-backed storage (à la Hasura migrations or Temporal workflows) — rollback and diffable.

  • Use Alembic-style migrations auto-generated from YAML diff.

  • Add schema test suites before applying migrations (validate field references, foreign keys, permissions).

2. AI & Vectorization Layer

Critique:
Native vectorization is forward-thinking but can become costly and inconsistent if every field can be embedded.

Suggestions:

  • Define vectorization policies in YAML:

  • Maintain a per-tenant embedding index via pgvector or Qdrant.

  • Use semantic caching to reduce token/compute cost.

  • Provide human review of embeddings for schema fields — not everything should be vectorized.

3. AI Development Workflow (Codex Loop)

Critique:
The Codex session lifecycle is clever — but it’s linear. AI projects benefit from branching and reinforcement loops.

Suggestions:

  • Add Codex Review Phase:

    • Auto-summarize JIRA & Confluence context

    • Run LLM-based “Architect Review” (is this secure? consistent? performant?)

    • Use GPT-based “Standards Agent” that checks PRs against coding guidelines.

  • Integrate with GitHub Copilot Enterprise / OpenAI MCP for round-trip context (MCP is ideal for JIRA ↔ Confluence ↔ GitHub orchestration).

  • Save Codex chat logs as embeddings to train your internal “Platform Architect Agent.”

4. Security & Compliance

Critique:
You’ve nailed AuthZ and RBAC — but this needs a full “tenant trust boundary” model early.

Suggestions:

  • Explicitly define Data Isolation Layers:

    • Logical tenant isolation (Row Level Security in Postgres)

    • Vector index partitioning

    • Blob access via SAS tokens scoped per MSP

  • Add Privacy Zones (e.g., “do not embed” fields flagged in YAML)

  • Compliance baseline: SOC2, GDPR, and DPA-ready from day 1.

5. Automation and Workflow

Critique:
Camunda and n8n are perfect choices — but you’ll need an abstraction layer to handle custom triggers/events.

Suggestions:

  • Introduce a “Trigger Gateway” microservice:

    • Converts DB events → NATS/Kafka messages → workflow inputs.

    • Allows low-code users to define automation logic as YAML, like:

  • Add AI Playbooks: Use LLMs to suggest workflows (“When a customer invoice is overdue, send a Teams reminder”).

6. End-User AI / MSP Copilot

Critique:
Giving MSPs and their clients access to NLP is a killer differentiator — but model alignment and persona control will matter.

Suggestions:

  • Add persona profiles:
    “Support Tech”, “Dispatcher”, “Account Manager”, “Client User”.

  • Define prompt templates and context packs per persona in YAML.

  • Allow MSP admins to audit conversations and retrain FAQs from chat logs.

  • Train a domain-tuned retrieval index using anonymized MSP data for “industry benchmark” answers.

7. Monitoring / Remote Access

Critique:
This is very hard to combine safely with multitenancy.

Suggestions:

  • Use lightweight remote agents with API key rotation and telemetry push (like ConnectWise Automate or NinjaOne agents).

  • Build agent events into the same workflow engine (e.g., “If agent offline for 30m → notify MSP”).

  • Consider OpenTelemetry as your baseline for metrics and distributed tracing.

8. Consumption-Based Billing

Critique:
Explaining usage is critical — and it’s hard to measure fair compute cost for AI ops.

Suggestions:

  • Create a “Billing Metadata Service” that logs:

    • Tokens used (OpenAI, Anthropic, local models)

    • Workflow executions

    • Storage and blob sizes

  • Provide AI-generated billing summaries to users (“You spent $18.22 on AI chat this week due to X workflows”).

  • Integrate with Azure Consumption APIs to pass through infra costs.

9. Benchmarking and Classification

Critique:
You’re thinking ahead — but standardized enums will be crucial for useful cross-MSP analytics.

Suggestions:

  • Maintain a global ontology registry:

  • AI can auto-map custom labels → standards for reporting.

  • Benchmark dashboards can use anonymized vectors to cluster MSPs by performance or efficiency traits.

10. MVP Recommendation

You could pilot this with a narrow but representative use case:

  • Start with “AI-Native Ticketing & Workflow” module:

    • YAML schema for tickets + notes + contacts

    • AI copilot that reads/write notes

    • Workflow triggers (e.g., auto-escalate stale tickets)

    • Built-in dashboard

    • Codex auto-dev + Confluence documentation loop

That slice exercises schema, AI, automation, RBAC, and Codex methodology — all core components in one ecosystem.

🚀 Next-Level Enhancements

Area

Idea

Benefit

AI Governance

Add an “AI Audit Trail” where every model decision (generation, vector update, schema suggestion) is logged with context.

Regulatory readiness and rollback safety

Open Integration

Adopt the Model Context Protocol (MCP) as the universal integration layer.

Interoperability between external copilots and internal services

Semantic Configs

Replace parts of YAML config with natural-language-to-YAML AI translation.

Non-technical admins can define automations

Agentic Extensions

Use MCP + RAG + LangGraph-style agent loops to let “Dev Agents” improve themselves based on prior Codex sessions.

Continuous learning and improvement

Local-first

Containerized dev environment mirrors Azure runtime.

Contributors can safely test AI/automation logic offline

If you’d like, I can turn this into a phase-by-phase AI architecture plan — starting with the “AI-native ticketing MVP,” mapping Codex flow, schema evolution, and automation triggers.
Would you like me to lay that out?

I want you to build out users, create a login screen, and build out navigation. The initial username is sdemulling and password is testing.

Customization

Vision

  • Views-as-first-class citizens
    Let users save named views (filters/sorts/columns/layout)—and share them. Pin a default org view; allow personal overrides.

  • WYSIWYG layout with limits
    Drag-and-drop grids/sections, but enforce a design system (spacing, typography, tokens) so every screen looks “on brand.”

  • Role- and context-aware visibility
    Component visibility by role, field values, device, tenant—keeps screens relevant and clean.

  • Data-bound controls + variables
    Add top-of-screen variables (date range, status, client) that drive every widget on the page—one screen, many uses.

  • Templates and a marketplace
    Ship great starting points (Ticket Triage, Dispatch Board, SLA Oversight). Invite vendors/partners to publish components/screens.

  • Personal workspaces
    Let users save entire screen layouts for their job-to-be-done (“Dispatcher”, “NOC”, “AM QBR”), and hot-swap them.

  • Safe extensibility
    Support custom components (like LWC/custom cards) in a sandbox with API-only data access; review and sign components.

  • Auditability
    Log who changed screens, when, and what changed. Allow rollbacks and version labels.

  • Performance guardrails
    Query budgets per component, skeleton loading, and “preview mode” to catch slow screens before publishing.

  • Shareable links & embeds
    Deep links to a view state (variables + filters) and secure embeds (e.g., in Teams/Confluence) drive adoption.

Tasks

  • Use familiar structure that users have already seen and have habits around.

    • Left hand navigation that can expand and collapse and has 3 levels. The nav structure will be oriented around user personas at an MSP.

    • Top bar contains msp logo, search, create button, buy/upgrade for admins, notifications, ai button, help button, community button, user button

    • Middle section for content

    • breadcrumb above the middle section to show navigation tree

  • Basic customizations

    • Can access under the settings → branding menu

    • Primary background color, secondary background color, foreground color and link color with dark mode and light modes built in.

    • Pick the logo for the top bar

  • Pods

    • Provide a collection of UI controls that are bound to data

    • Each control has a label to explain what it is

    • Basic controls to start.

      • Textbox

      • Textarea

      • Number

      • Decimal

      • Currency

      • Dropdown / selection

      • Checkbox

    • Can specify the number of columns, or make a flexible pod that wraps controls

  • Screen layouts (Airtable, Salesforce, Notion)

  • Example Layout

Notes: {{#each ticket_notes}} - {{this.created_at}} by {{this.author_id}}: {{this.body}} {{/each}} context: ticket: { source: dataSources.tickets, where: { id: "$context.ticket_id" } } ticket_notes: { source: dataSources.ticket_notes } output.bind: "$widgets.ai_summary.text" - id: triage_macro label: "Propose Next Steps" prompt: | Given the ticket details and KB context, propose 3 next steps with justification. retrieval: top_kb: type: vector index: "kb_articles_vec" query: "$widgets.search.input || $data.tickets.title" top_k: 5 - type: textBlock id: ai_summary title: "AI Summary" - type: marketplace.component ref: "vendor.acme.kbPanel@1.3.2" inputs: tenant_id: "$vars.tenant_id" ticket_id: "$context.ticket_id" rbac: visibility: { permission: "kb:view" } theming: scheme: "system" # light/dark/system tokens: density: "comfortable" accent: "blue" performance: budgets: maxNetworkRequests: 12 maxQueryTimeMsP95: 1800 skeletons: table: true form: true audit: enabled: true redact: fields: ["ticket_notes.body"] # example PII/PHI redaction retention: days: 365 export: deepLink: enabled: true includeVariables: true ttl_minutes: 60 embed: allowIn: ["teams","confluence"] signedUrl: true ]] >

Database

  • Postgres

  • Schema designed as a YAML

  • Schema is automatically checked and updated from YAML on login

  • Customized schema is also defined as YAML

  • SCHEMA must be up to date

  • Once tables and fields are defined, it's really bad to remove them. Deprecation strategies must allow for 1 year, and it can be undone.

  • Text fields can be vectorized

  • Blobs should not be in the database. they are links to Blob Storage through hashkeys. If an image is used twice, it's one blob.

  • Permissions are record level to filter what is available to AI

  • It's multitenant capable so MSP's unique id's are in the table. In practice, this will be used for datareporting and franchises/opcos.

  • Custom fields must be in a separate table per MSP. If a table is called customers, make a customers_custom table, defined by YAML.

Provisioning

  • Provisioning service accepts an email address to restrict who the first user can be

  • Provisioning will contact a content service to get base packages and functionality

Basic environment setup

  • Local container and azure versions

  • Azure blob storage

  • Azure Postgres

  • Azure app service

  • Python back end code

  • React front end

  • Material ui framework

  • Security and compliance. RBAC permissions, AuthZ, GDAP and other compliance needs for EU, AU, NA are built in. Multilingual and internationalization are built in. Audit trails are built in. Versioning is built in. Retention is built in.

  • AI. Data should be vectorized natively. Expect conversational NLP to be native in the platform. AI should be accessible in copilot, teams, and elsewhere.

  • Automation. Data triggers should kick off a workflow engine that allows a customer to automate processes. A playbook / runbook approach should be used to define a manual flowfirst, and then to automate peices of it. Camunda, N8N are good examples of workflow engines.   • Built in coding. Screens can be created using YAML and Python. Backend services can created this way as well. Everything will be reviewed by AI for standards and security. Versioning is built in.

  • Built in dashboarding and reporting. Dashboards are meant to see live data, and navigate to screens. Reporting is deep analysis, often involving month over month and trends.

  • Built in benchmarking. Data in aggregate means you can compare your performance against those of others.

  • Built in segmentation and classification. Customization is possible, but must fall within certain standards. for example, Ticket status can have many names, but falls into New, In Progress, and Done. Healthcare represents and industry. This strategy is to ensure benchmarking works.

  • Bring your user. Users can access multiple MSP’s, and bring their skills and awards with them. gamification to some degree.

  • Consumption based billing. Pay for what you use. But we need to highly explain compute, storage, tokens, and other charges on demand.

  • Global Search. Can be done with traditional keyword or by asking questions

Design order

  1. https://sdemulling.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/AI/pages/2424833/Environment?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiMWNhZmZlOWJhMGEwNDk0N2JhMjQ5ZmQ1NzBhYWNhYjQiLCJwIjoiYyJ9

  2. https://sdemulling.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/AI/pages/2490369/Database?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiNWYzODY3ODM5MGJiNDIzNDgwMTViY2MyOGNmZGQzMDMiLCJwIjoiYyJ9

  3. https://sdemulling.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/AI/pages/2293773/Users?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiYjNlMDA1MWJmNDlhNDU1NzgyYmU2YTgzZmJlYTc3MmUiLCJwIjoiYyJ9

  4. https://sdemulling.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/AI/pages/2424855/Provisioning?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiYzBjOGM1Yjk4YjIxNDJiMTg0MzMxY2E0ZWJlYzU5OTMiLCJwIjoiYyJ9

  5. https://sdemulling.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/AI/pages/2392084/Views+Customization?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiZWFlYmJmYzNhODg1NDU0YTllODg5MTg2ODFjYTEyODEiLCJwIjoiYyJ9

Database

  • Postgres

  • Schema designed as a YAML

  • Schema is automatically checked and updated from YAML on login

  • Customized schema is also defined as YAML

  • SCHEMA must be up to date

  • Once tables and fields are defined, it's really bad to remove them. Deprecation strategies must allow for 1 year, and it can be undone.

  • Text fields can be vectorized

  • Blobs should not be in the database. they are links to Blob Storage through hashkeys. If an image is used twice, it's one blob.

  • Permissions are record level to filter what is available to AI

  • It's multitenant capable so MSP's unique id's are in the table. In practice, this will be used for datareporting and franchises/opcos.

  • Custom fields must be in a separate table per MSP. If a table is called customers, make a customers_custom table, defined by YAML.

Basic environment setup

  • Local container and azure versions

  • Azure blob storage

  • Azure Postgres

  • Azure app service

  • Python back end code

  • React front end

  • Material ui framework

Development Methodology - AI Driven

  • Start a codex session

  • Identify the next item to work on from the Design order section

  • Create a JIRA Epic for each Codex initial request

  • Create a JIRA task associated with that epic for each code change

  • Create automated testing for every request

  • Create a confluence page explaining what was done, relevant screenshots and how it might benefit MSPs.

  • Human in the loop verification and testing, with possible iterative requirements, added as a story.

  • Update the confluence, human approves,

  • Update Github

  • Provide notes for future codex sessions that would be beneficial

  • codex session completed.

You are developing a platform application for MSP’s.

  1. An overview of the vision and plan can be found in c:\source\platform\plan\

  2. Context notes from previous sessions can be found under C:\source\platform\context

  3. Log your work under C:\source\platform\logs into CSV’s that can be imported into Jira.

  4. Create unit testing and automation as needed

  5. Document your work under C:\source\platform\documentation so that it can be imported into confluence.

  6. Keep context notes for future iterations under C:\source\platform\context

Vision

We are creating a MSP SaaS platform that has AI at its foundation. Thie main pillars of this platform include:

  • Highly customizable. Customers must be able to create their own tables and fields to support their own business. The UI must be flexible in allowing the customer to display information as they see fit and to develop no code lines of business content.

  • Notifications. A quick way to let a user know important information.

  • Community. Sharing securely is important. We assume a peer model where communities can come together andd share their fields and customizations. Vendors are a type of community. It is expected that users may have multiple access to different systems with different permisssions in each.

  • Security and compliance. RBAC permissions, AuthZ, GDAP and other compliance needs for EU, AU, NA are built in. Multilingual and internationalization are built in. Audit trails are built in. Versioning is built in. Retention is built in.

  • AI. Data should be vectorized natively. Expect conversational NLP to be native in the platform. AI should be accessible in copilot, teams, and elsewhere.

  • Automation. Data triggers should kick off a workflow engine that allows a customer to automate processes. A playbook / runbook approach should be used to define a manual flowfirst, and then to automate peices of it. Camunda, N8N are good examples of workflow engines. Should connect to everything microsoft, email, sharepoint, teams.

  • Monitoring. Customers will sell software and solutions to their customers. We must be able to remotely access the system to install software, support it, and ensure its health.

  • End user AI and chat. We will also deliver end user NLP for an MSP's clients

  • Easy to integrate. REST APIs, MCPs are always delivered and support customizations. Spreadsheet and other data imports are common and should be a foundational task customers can do. Vendor API's should be easily imported and usable.

  • Built in coding. Screens can be created using YAML and Python. Backend services can created this way as well. Everything will be reviewed by AI for standards and security. Versioning is built in.

  • Built in dashboarding and reporting. Dashboards are meant to see live data, and navigate to screens. Reporting is deep analysis, often involving month over month and trends.

  • Built in benchmarking. Data in aggregate means you can compare your performance against those of others.

  • Built in segmentation and classification. Customization is possible, but must fall within certain standards. for example, Ticket status can have many names, but falls into New, In Progress, and Done. Healthcare represents and industry. This strategy is to ensure benchmarking works.

  • Bring your user. Users can access multiple MSP’s, and bring their skills and awards with them. gamification to some degree.

  • Consumption based billing. Pay for what you use. But we need to highly explain compute, storage, tokens, and other charges on demand.

  • Global Search. Can be done with traditional keyword or by asking questions

Design order

Basic environment setup

  • Local container and azure versions

  • Azure blob storage

  • Azure Postgres

  • Azure app service

  • Python back end code

  • React front end

  • Material ui framework

Database

  • Postgres - would SIEM work

  • Schema designed as a YAML

  • Schema is automatically checked and updated from YAML on login

  • Customized schema is also defined as YAML

  • SCHEMA must be up to date

  • Once tables and fields are defined, it's really bad to remove them. Deprecation strategies must allow for 1 year, and it can be undone.

  • Text fields can be vectorized

  • Blobs should not be in the database. they are links to Blob Storage through hashkeys. If an image is used twice, it's one blob.

  • Permissions are record level to filter what is available to AI

  • It's multitenant capable so MSP's unique id's are in the table. In practice, this will be used for datareporting and franchises/opcos.

  • Custom fields must be in a separate table per MSP. If a table is called customers, make a customers_custom table, defined by YAML.

User Structure

  • Username and password must be securely encrypted and support 2FA

  • Picture, first name, last name, phone number, email, teamsid, allowsms, allowteams, isowner

  • Email and phone number must be verified

  • Create a temporary login page

  • Create user can be called from login. First user is the owner.

  • There is only one owner, it's a special user

Provisioning

  • Provisioning service accepts an email address to restrict who the first user can be

  • Provisioning will contact a content service to get base packages and functionality

Customization

Vision

  • Views-as-first-class citizens
    Let users save named views (filters/sorts/columns/layout)—and share them. Pin a default org view; allow personal overrides.

  • WYSIWYG layout with limits
    Drag-and-drop grids/sections, but enforce a design system (spacing, typography, tokens) so every screen looks “on brand.”

  • Role- and context-aware visibility
    Component visibility by role, field values, device, tenant—keeps screens relevant and clean.

  • Data-bound controls + variables
    Add top-of-screen variables (date range, status, client) that drive every widget on the page—one screen, many uses.

  • Templates and a marketplace
    Ship great starting points (Ticket Triage, Dispatch Board, SLA Oversight). Invite vendors/partners to publish components/screens.

  • Personal workspaces
    Let users save entire screen layouts for their job-to-be-done (“Dispatcher”, “NOC”, “AM QBR”), and hot-swap them.

  • Safe extensibility
    Support custom components (like LWC/custom cards) in a sandbox with API-only data access; review and sign components.

  • Auditability
    Log who changed screens, when, and what changed. Allow rollbacks and version labels.

  • Performance guardrails
    Query budgets per component, skeleton loading, and “preview mode” to catch slow screens before publishing.

  • Shareable links & embeds
    Deep links to a view state (variables + filters) and secure embeds (e.g., in Teams/Confluence) drive adoption.

Tasks

  • Use familiar structure that users have already seen and have habits around.

    • Left hand navigation that can expand and collapse and has 3 levels. The nav structure will be oriented around user personas at an MSP.

    • Top bar contains msp logo, search, create button, buy/upgrade for admins, notifications, ai button, help button, community button, user button

    • Middle section for content

    • breadcrumb above the middle section to show navigation tree

  • Basic customizations

    • Can access under the settings → branding menu

    • Primary background color, secondary background color, foreground color and link color with dark mode and light modes built in.

    • Pick the logo for the top bar

  • Pods

    • Provide a collection of UI controls that are bound to data

    • Each control has a label to explain what it is

    • Basic controls to start.

      • Textbox

      • Textarea

      • Number

      • Decimal

      • Currency

      • Dropdown / selection

      • Checkbox

    • Can specify the number of columns, or make a flexible pod that wraps controls

  • Screen layouts (Airtable, Salesforce, Notion)

    • 16 columns

  • Example Layout

yamlwide1800= :since.start AND t.created_at <= :since.end ORDER BY t.priority DESC, t.sla_due_at ASC rls: true # rely on DB row-level security cache: ttl_seconds: 15 vary_on: [tenant_id, status, owner, since] budgets: max_rows: 1000 timeout_ms: 1800 ticket_search: type: hybrid pipeline: - bm25: index: "tickets_fts" query_from: "$widgets.search.input" limit: 500 - vector: index: "tickets_vec" embedder: "e5-small-512" ef_search: 80 top_k: 100 - rerank: model: "cross-encoder-mini" top_k: 30 # For AI summaries; retrieves the rich note history ticket_notes: type: sql read: query: | SELECT n.ticket_id, n.body, n.author_id, n.created_at FROM ticket_notes n WHERE n.ticket_id = :context.ticket_id ORDER BY n.created_at DESC rls: true budgets: { max_rows: 200 } actions: assign_ticket: type: mutation.sql query: "UPDATE tickets SET owner_id = :owner_id WHERE id = :ticket_id AND tenant_id = :tenant_id" rbac: { permission: "tickets:assign" } audit: message: "Assigned ticket {{ticket_id}} to {{owner_id}}" close_ticket: type: mutation.workflow workflow: "n8n://flows/close-ticket" # or camunda://... input: ticket_id: ":ticket_id" tenant_id: ":tenant_id" rbac: { permission: "tickets:close" } layout: grid: columns: 12 gutter: 12 regions: - id: header row: 1 col: 1 colSpan: 12 widgets: - type: variablesBar # renders variables defined above - type: kpis items: - label: "Open" source.sql: "SELECT count(*) FROM tickets WHERE tenant_id=:tenant_id AND status <> 'Done'" - label: "Breaching in 4h" source.sql: | SELECT count(*) FROM tickets WHERE tenant_id=:tenant_id AND status NOT IN ('Done') AND sla_due_at < now() + interval '4 hours' - id: left row: 2 col: 1 colSpan: 7 widgets: - id: search type: searchBox placeholder: "Search titles, notes, customers…" binds.to: dataSources.ticket_search - id: table type: table title: "Tickets" data: dataSources.tickets columns: - { key: number, width: 120, fixed: left } - User Structure

  • Username and password must be securely encrypted and support 2FA

  • Picture, first name, last name, phone number, email, teamsid, allowsms, allowteams, isowner

This is a fantastically ambitious and well-structured vision, and honestly, it reads like an evolved blueprint for the “Next-Gen ConnectWise Platform.” It’s modular, highly extensible, AI-native, and clearly written from real product pain points you’ve lived through. Below is a structured critique and a set of enhancements that could increase your chance of long-term success, technically, organizationally, and strategically.

🧭 Overall Vision Feedback

Strengths

  • MSP-centric clarity: You’ve captured the realities of multi-tenant MSP environments (franchises, opcos, multi-MSP identities) beautifully. This will resonate immediately with the ecosystem.

  • Data-centric + AI-native: Vectorization, conversational NLP, and integrated automation show that AI isn’t a bolt-on — it’s foundational.

  • Composable extensibility: The “custom tables via YAML” model is brilliant for building a PaaS-like MSP system.

  • Human-in-the-loop AI ops: The Codex-driven dev workflow bridges AI-generated work and human QA — perfect for iterative safety.

Potential Risks

  • Platform scope creep — you’re essentially combining Notion, ServiceNow, ConnectWise, and Retool into one AI-native platform. To make it real, you’ll need a strong MVP slice.

  • Complexity of schema versioning + customization — even ServiceNow struggles here. Dynamic schema updates, YAML validation, and vector indexing together will be tricky.

  • AI safety + hallucination risk — “AI reviewing AI-generated code” and “AI defining schemas” require strict guardrails (LLM self-review loops + unit tests won’t be enough alone).

🧩 Architecture Critique & Recommendations

1. Data Model (YAML Schema Design)

Critique:
YAML-defined schemas are powerful, but dynamic migrations and tenant isolation require absolute rigor.

Suggestions:

  • Implement a Schema Controller service (like Kubernetes CRDs): validates, version-controls, and applies YAML schemas via APIs.

  • Store schema versions in Git-backed storage (à la Hasura migrations or Temporal workflows) — rollback and diffable.

  • Use Alembic-style migrations auto-generated from YAML diff.

  • Add schema test suites before applying migrations (validate field references, foreign keys, permissions).

2. AI & Vectorization Layer

Critique:
Native vectorization is forward-thinking but can become costly and inconsistent if every field can be embedded.

Suggestions:

  • Define vectorization policies in YAML:

  • Maintain a per-tenant embedding index via pgvector or Qdrant.

  • Use semantic caching to reduce token/compute cost.

  • Provide human review of embeddings for schema fields — not everything should be vectorized.

3. AI Development Workflow (Codex Loop)

Critique:
The Codex session lifecycle is clever — but it’s linear. AI projects benefit from branching and reinforcement loops.

Suggestions:

  • Add Codex Review Phase:

    • Auto-summarize JIRA & Confluence context

    • Run LLM-based “Architect Review” (is this secure? consistent? performant?)

    • Use GPT-based “Standards Agent” that checks PRs against coding guidelines.

  • Integrate with GitHub Copilot Enterprise / OpenAI MCP for round-trip context (MCP is ideal for JIRA ↔ Confluence ↔ GitHub orchestration).

  • Save Codex chat logs as embeddings to train your internal “Platform Architect Agent.”

4. Security & Compliance

Critique:
You’ve nailed AuthZ and RBAC — but this needs a full “tenant trust boundary” model early.

Suggestions:

  • Explicitly define Data Isolation Layers:

    • Logical tenant isolation (Row Level Security in Postgres)

    • Vector index partitioning

    • Blob access via SAS tokens scoped per MSP

  • Add Privacy Zones (e.g., “do not embed” fields flagged in YAML)

  • Compliance baseline: SOC2, GDPR, and DPA-ready from day 1.

5. Automation and Workflow

Critique:
Camunda and n8n are perfect choices — but you’ll need an abstraction layer to handle custom triggers/events.

Suggestions:

  • Introduce a “Trigger Gateway” microservice:

    • Converts DB events → NATS/Kafka messages → workflow inputs.

    • Allows low-code users to define automation logic as YAML, like:

  • Add AI Playbooks: Use LLMs to suggest workflows (“When a customer invoice is overdue, send a Teams reminder”).

6. End-User AI / MSP Copilot

Critique:
Giving MSPs and their clients access to NLP is a killer differentiator — but model alignment and persona control will matter.

Suggestions:

  • Add persona profiles:
    “Support Tech”, “Dispatcher”, “Account Manager”, “Client User”.

  • Define prompt templates and context packs per persona in YAML.

  • Allow MSP admins to audit conversations and retrain FAQs from chat logs.

  • Train a domain-tuned retrieval index using anonymized MSP data for “industry benchmark” answers.

7. Monitoring / Remote Access

Critique:
This is very hard to combine safely with multitenancy.

Suggestions:

  • Use lightweight remote agents with API key rotation and telemetry push (like ConnectWise Automate or NinjaOne agents).

  • Build agent events into the same workflow engine (e.g., “If agent offline for 30m → notify MSP”).

  • Consider OpenTelemetry as your baseline for metrics and distributed tracing.

8. Consumption-Based Billing

Critique:
Explaining usage is critical — and it’s hard to measure fair compute cost for AI ops.

Suggestions:

  • Create a “Billing Metadata Service” that logs:

    • Tokens used (OpenAI, Anthropic, local models)

    • Workflow executions

    • Storage and blob sizes

  • Provide AI-generated billing summaries to users (“You spent $18.22 on AI chat this week due to X workflows”).

  • Integrate with Azure Consumption APIs to pass through infra costs.

9. Benchmarking and Classification

Critique:
You’re thinking ahead — but standardized enums will be crucial for useful cross-MSP analytics.

Suggestions:

  • Maintain a global ontology registry:

  • AI can auto-map custom labels → standards for reporting.

  • Benchmark dashboards can use anonymized vectors to cluster MSPs by performance or efficiency traits.

10. MVP Recommendation

You could pilot this with a narrow but representative use case:

  • Start with “AI-Native Ticketing & Workflow” module:

    • YAML schema for tickets + notes + contacts

    • AI copilot that reads/write notes

    • Workflow triggers (e.g., auto-escalate stale tickets)

    • Built-in dashboard

    • Codex auto-dev + Confluence documentation loop

That slice exercises schema, AI, automation, RBAC, and Codex methodology — all core components in one ecosystem.

🚀 Next-Level Enhancements

Area

Idea

Benefit

AI Governance

Add an “AI Audit Trail” where every model decision (generation, vector update, schema suggestion) is logged with context.

Regulatory readiness and rollback safety

Open Integration

Adopt the Model Context Protocol (MCP) as the universal integration layer.

Interoperability between external copilots and internal services

Semantic Configs

Replace parts of YAML config with natural-language-to-YAML AI translation.

Non-technical admins can define automations

Agentic Extensions

Use MCP + RAG + LangGraph-style agent loops to let “Dev Agents” improve themselves based on prior Codex sessions.

Continuous learning and improvement

Local-first

Containerized dev environment mirrors Azure runtime.

Contributors can safely test AI/automation logic offline

If you’d like, I can turn this into a phase-by-phase AI architecture plan — starting with the “AI-native ticketing MVP,” mapping Codex flow, schema evolution, and automation triggers.
Would you like me to lay that out?

Vision

We are creating a MSP SaaS platform that has AI at its foundation. Thie main pillars of this platform include:

  • Highly customizable. Customers must be able to create their own tables and fields to support their own business. The UI must be flexible in allowing the customer to display information as they see fit and to develop no code lines of business content.

  • Community. Sharing securely is important. We assume a peer model where communities can come together andd share their fields and customizations. Vend ors are a type of community. It is expected that users may have multiple access to different systems with different permisssions in each.

  • Security and compliance. RBAC permissions, AuthZ, GDAP and other compliance needs for EU, AU, NA are built in. Multilingual and internationalization are built in. Audit trails are built in. Versioning is built in. Retention is built in.

  • AI. Data should be vectorized natively. Expect conversational NLP to be native in the platform. AI should be accessible in copilot, teams, and elsewhere.

  • Automation. Data triggers should kick off a workflow engine that allows a customer to automate processes. A playbook / runbook approach should be used to define a manual flowfirst, and then to automate peices of it. Camunda, N8N are good examples of workflow engines. Should connect to everything microsoft, email, sharepoint, teams.

  • Monitoring. Customers will sell software and solutions to their customers. We must be able to remotely access the system to install software, support it, and ensure it's health.

  • End user AI and chat. We will also deliver end user NLP for an MSP's clients

  • Easy to integrate. REST APIs, MCPs are always delivered and support customizations. Spreadsheet and other data imports are common and should be a foundational task customers can do. Vendor API's should be easily imported and usable.

  • Built in coding. Screens can be created using YAML and Python. Backend services can created this way as well. Everything will be reviewed by AI for standards and security. Versioning is built in.

  • Built in dashboarding and reporting. Dashboards are meant to see live data, and navigate to screens. Reporting is deep analysis, often involving month over month and trends.

  • Built in benchmarking. Data in aggregate means you can compare your performance against those of others.

  • Built in segmentation and classification. Customization is possible, but must fall within certain standards. for example, Ticket status can have many names, but falls into New, In Progress, and Done. Healthcare represents and industry. This strategy is to ensure benchmarking works.

  • Bring your user. Users can access multiple MSP’s, and bring their skills and awards with them.

  • Consumption based billing. Pay for what you use. But we need to highly explain compute, storage, tokens, and other charges on demand.

  • Global Search. Can be done with traditional keyword or by asking questions

Development Methodology - AI Driven

  • Start a codex session

  • Identify the next item to work on from the Design order section

  • Create a JIRA Epic for each Codex initial request

  • Create a JIRA task associated with that epic for each code change

  • Create automated testing for every request

  • Create a confluence page explaining what was done, relevant screenshots and how it might benefit MSPs.

  • Human in the loop verification and testing, with possible iterative requirements, added as a story.

  • Update the confluence, human approves,

  • Update Github

  • Provide notes for future codex sessions that would be benificial

  • codex session completed.

Design order

Basic environment setup

  • Local container and azure versions

  • Azure blob storage

  • Azure Postgres

  • Azure app service

  • Python back end code

  • React front end

  • Material ui framework

Database

  • Postgres - would SIEM work

  • Schema designed as a YAML

  • Schema is automatically checked and updated from YAML on login

  • Customized schema is also defined as YAML

  • SCHEMA must be up to date

  • Once tables and fields are defined, it's really bad to remove them. Deprecation strategies must allow for 1 year, and it can be undone.

  • Text fields can be vectorized

  • Blobs should not be in the database. they are links to Blob Storage through hashkeys. If an image is used twice, it's one blob.

  • Permissions are record level to filter what is available to AI

  • It's multitenant capable so MSP's unique id's are in the table. In practice, this will be used for datareporting and franchises/opcos.

  • Custom fields must be in a separate table per MSP. If a table is called customers, make a customers_custom table, defined by YAML.

User Structure

  • Username and password must be securely encrypted and support 2FA

  • Picture, first name, last name, phone number, email, teamsid, allowsms, allowteams, isowner

  • Email and phone number must be verified

  • Create a temporary login page

  • Create user can be called from login. First user is the owner.

  • There is only one owner, it's a special user

Provisioning

  • Provisioning service accepts an email address to restrict who the first user can be

  • Provisioning will contact a content service to get base packages and functionality

Customization

  • Use familiar structure that users have already seen and have habits around.

    • Left hand navigation that can expand and collapse and has 3 levels. The nav structure will be oriented around user personas at an MSP.

    • Top bar contains msp logo, search, create button, buy/upgrade for admins, notifications, ai button, help button, community button, user button

    • Middle section for content

    • breadcrumb above the middle section to show navigation tree

  • Basic customizations

    • Primary color and secondary color with dark mode and light modes built in.

    • Pick the logo for the top bar
